pattern: Enumerating non-decreasing factorizations into exactly k parts and tracking the tightest spread finds a split whose largest and smallest factors are as close as possible.
hints: Because the factors multiply to a fixed value, you are searching over the divisors and want the chosen parts clustered near the kth root.

Build the factor list in non-decreasing order so each recursive choice only considers divisors at least as large as the previous one, pruning when the smallest possible remaining factors would already exceed what is left.

Recurse choosing the next factor d that divides the remaining product, requiring d to the power of the remaining count to fit within the remainder; at the last slot the leftover is forced, and you keep whichever complete split minimizes max minus min.

edge_inputs.description: perfect square split into two equal halves
three factors with a forced large tail

stress.description: highly composite numbers with small k for many valid splits
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    bases = [720, 840, 1260, 2520, 5040, 720720 % 100000 + 4, 36, 96, 144, 360]
    n = rng.choice(bases)
    if n < 4:
        n = 36
    # count divisors to keep k strictly below it
    divs = sum(1 for d in range(1, n + 1) if n % d == 0)
    k = rng.randrange(2, min(5, divs - 1) + 1)
    return [n, k]
stress.note: k kept strictly below the divisor count
